[165:47]The deal's already being signed by the Treasury Secretary Scott Besant.

[166:13]to Russia recently. At the same time, the United States voted with Russia and 18 other countries

[166:28]which is crazy. So these are some of the developments. A lot of movement is happening on

[168:12]between Washington and Moscow. And Trump is very aggressively courting Russia for a deal.

[169:27]and to Taiwan, to the Indo-Pacific region.

[169:41]without a real strategy. And again, I wonder what is it that we're actually getting in exchange

[170:51]of the gate.

[185:01]the center right party in Germany.

[185:14]And

[185:15]The result was really shocking because AFD has doubled their vote get from the previous election.

[187:54]We need to protect the borders better. We must regain control over those who come into

[187:59]our country. So this is the result in Germany. And what is important to explain about Germany

[188:08]is that their constitution effectively bans far left and far right politics. There is

[188:23]Which is at the core of the German constitution, which basically makes it illegal

[188:30]for there to be a far left political party or a far right political party.

[188:35]They cannot be too far left or too far right.

[188:37]They can't be the communists because of the influence of the communists in East Germany during the Cold War.

[188:44]but they also can't be too far right because of the Nazis. So it is literally, according to their

[188:51]constitution, only allowable to be somewhat in the center, center right, center left.

[188:59]And this became an issue in 2020 when the AFD party had a very far right faction.

[189:07]which comprised 20% of their party. It was identitarian, racialist, anti-immigrant.

[189:15]And it was literally made illegal by the German courts. They said it had to be disbanded.

[189:21]And so formally they had to disband the group. And then the AFD had to balance itself with

[189:27]a more center-right libertarian element. They said it's in the middle, it's in the middle

[189:34]which was focused more on the Euro, the European Union, economic issues.

[189:42]And so when you look at a country like Germany, when you consider Europe,

[189:47]it is fundamentally different than the United States.

[189:51]In the United States, there is no constitutional limit on how far right

[189:54]a party can be. And it's rhetoric and it's policies.

[189:59]In Germany, there is. But there's also a political reality.

[190:03]All of the major parties, the left-wing and right-wing governing parties, the center-right,

[190:08]center-left parties, have created a political firewall.

[190:13]They say they are unwilling to form government with far-left and far-right parties like

[190:19]the AFD.

[190:21]So even though the far left or far right might have a large turnout and might have a lot

[190:27]of seats in the legislature, and maybe it might be easier to govern with them than without

[190:33]They will not form a government with those parties. They have something similar in France and we saw that during the French elections last year

[190:41]when Marine Le Pen's National Rally Party

[190:46]Was the most popular party in France, but the center right and the left-wing parties came together to deny them a first-place finish

[190:54]and for them to take over the French legislature

[190:59]And they call it something different in France, but it's the same principle.

[191:02]They do, there is a majority opinion, there's a political will to keep the far right out of power.

[191:09]And so in Germany, it is legal and it is political

[191:13]that makes it virtually impossible

[191:15]for the far right to govern.

[191:16]So they're up against a lot of stiff resistance.

[191:19]In spite of this, in spite of this political unwillingness

[191:23]to put them in the government,

[191:24]in spite of the German government's attacks on the party,

[191:33]And again, they're not going to be a part of the government, the center right is going

[192:34]the new chancellor, this MERS,

[194:18]you can never have the far right win.

[194:21]And this is what I wanted to talk about tonight.

[194:25]This is really the crux of what I have been talking about for as long as I've been doing

[194:32]this show, which is we are the far right.

[194:36]We are identitarian.

[194:38]We believe that there is an ethnic or a racial basis for our nationhood, for our culture.

[194:45]There is something connected there.

[194:48]We believe in preserving a traditional national identity and culture.

[194:54]We believe in traditional values.

[194:56]We believe in industrial policy, national sovereignty,

[195:00]which means that our national governments have control

[195:03]and don't delegate it to supernational institutions.

[195:07]We believe in protecting our industry from free trade

[195:09]or against globalization.

[195:11]All these things.

[195:12]That is a distinct political philosophy.

[195:16]And you could call it nationalism, you could call it, you know, populist nationalism, national conservatism, whatever.

[195:23]But it is a distinct worldview.

[195:25]It is different than conservatism.

[195:30]Conservatism does not have an ethnic basis for nationhood. It is not concerned with national

[195:36]identity. It does not want to impose the Christian religion on its people. It is in favor of a small

[195:44]central government, limited government, individualism, free trade, globalization,

[195:49]financialization. It is a distinct philosophy. And so you have the far right, nationalists,

[195:59]Now, who controls the institutions of the opposition?

[196:04]If the liberal progressive leftists, if the center left is hegemonic, if they are in power,

[196:13]If the center, left or progressives control the transatlantic institutions, if they are who is in power in Washington, in the federal government, in Hollywood, on Wall Street, in American media, if they are in power at the United Nations, at the European Union, in London, in Brussels, in Paris, in Berlin, and that is who is in power.

[196:40]The opposition to the hegemonic power is controlled by the center-right conservatives, the Republican

[196:49]party, the Tories, the center-right parties in Western Europe, the conservative media,

[196:57]these conservative connecting institutions with their backing from the Zionists from

[197:03]That is the political makeup of these countries.

[197:06]You have your hegemonic center left,

[197:08]hegemonic progressive left, center left liberals

[197:12]from Washington, from Brussels, from London,

[197:15]from really the American transatlantic structure.

[197:22]And then the controlled opposition are the conservatives.

[197:26]That is the structure.

[197:28]And the question for the far right
